meta.title: F-Type Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.tagPill: JAGUAR • F-TYPE • 2013–2024 • 1 Generation • AJ126 V6 • AJ133 V8 • AJ20D6
hero.h1: F-Type Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every F-Type engine, from the supercharged AJ126 V6 to the thunderous AJ133 V8 and the late AJ20D6 straight-six. All generations, real UK reliability data, failure patterns and honest replacement economics — backed by 50+ genuine UK F-Type enquiries in 2025 [JAG-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ✅ Safe — The F-Type rule: a low-mileage example easily justifies an AJ133 rebuild, but a £10k car with a £9k bill needs thinking through
AJ20D6 — the late 3.0 straight-six; far fewer cooling-system failures than its V6 predecessor
AJ126 3.0 V6 SC — cooling system failures are well-documented, though rarely catastrophic if caught early
2019+ F-Type with the AJ20D6 straight-six — the cleanest reliability record and modern engineering
AJ126 cooling system / water pump — 560 enquiries across all AJ126 models [JAG-VERIFIED]

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The F-Type is Jaguar's most compelling modern sports car — but buy a 2019+ car with the straight-six if you want the cleanest reliability record, and budget for cooling-system maintenance on any V6 or V8.

rows.value: AJ126 3.0 V6 SC — 560 enquiries across all AJ126 models [JAG-VERIFIED]
AJ20D6 — 3.0 straight-six; clean record with no systemic failure pattern [PATTERN DATA]
AJ126 3.0 V6 SC — cooling system and crossover pipe failures [PATTERN DATA]

generations.h2: F-Type — One Generation, Three Powertrain Eras
generations.subHeadline: The X152 ran from 2013 to 2024 with one chassis but three distinct engine families — choose by reliability as much as soundtrack.

cards.title: X152 (2013–2024) — The F-Type's only generation
cards.verdict: The sound that made the F-Type famous. The AJ133 V8 is glorious — but pre-2019 cars need a cooling-system inspection before you buy, and the AJ126's plastic crossover pipes are a known weak point. Budget £1,000–£2,000 for cooling-system preventative work if history is unclear.

cards.title: 2019–2024: Revised V8 + Straight-Six Era
cards.verdict: The car Jaguar should have built from the start. The AJ20D6 straight-six ditches the V6's cooling-system weak points for a clean reliability record, and the revised V8 carries over the 2013+ tensioner improvements. Buy a 2019+ car with the straight-six if reliability is your priority — you lose none of the character.

engineDatabase.h2: F-Type Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine ever fitted to the Jaguar F-Type X152 — verified against OEM sources.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: AJ126, AJ133 and AJ20D6 ratings: High confidence — based on 560+ [JAG-VERIFIED] enquiries across the AJ126 and AJ133 engine families, cross-referenced with specialist interviews and documented failure patterns. AJ20D6 rating: Expert-knowledge tier — documented pattern data and specialist interviews, with no proprietary volume data specific to the F-Type straight-six. Data note (internal): * Correction applied: The original data file listed 306DT (2.7/3.0 TDV6 diesel) as an F-Type engine. This was corrected → no diesel engine was ever fitted to the F-Type. Removed from engine list. * Correction applied: 508PN was initially listed as an F-Type engine code. Corrected → 508PN is the naturally aspirated 5.0 V8 (used in XK, XJ). The F-Type uses AJ133 (supercharged 5.0 V8), not 508PN. 508PN has been removed from the F-Type engine list. * AJ20D6 verification: Confirmed via live verification that the 3.0 straight-six Ingenium (AJ20D6) was introduced to the F-Type in 2019 (facelift) as the replacement for the AJ126 V6. Power outputs: P300 (300bhp), P340 (340bhp), P400 (400bhp). This is correctly attributed as the F-Type's late-generation engine.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The six most frequent F-Type failure patterns — what to check, which engines they affect, and what they cost.

problems.issue: AJ126 Cooling System Crossover Pipe Failure
problems.description: The plastic coolant crossover pipes on the supercharged 3.0 V6 crack with age and heat cycling. These engines cannot tolerate overheating without warping heads or damaging the supercharger. Pre-2016 cars are highest risk.

The 5.0 V8's early-build weak point — chain tensioner design was revised in 2013. F-Type production started in 2013, so most F-Types are post-revision, but early 2013 cars without documented tensioner history need inspection.

A well-known shared quirk of the AJ126 and AJ133 — the coupler between the supercharger and drive pulley wears, producing a rattling noise on idle. Rarely catastrophic on its own, but replacement involves supercharger removal.
The water pump on the supercharged V6 has a documented failure pattern — leaks or seizes without warning. Pre-2016 cars are most affected. Replacement cost: £600–£1,200 [JAG-QUOTE].

problems.issue: AJ133 Cooling System Crossover Pipe Leaks
problems.description: Same plastic crossover pipe weakness as the AJ126, but on the 5.0 V8. Later (2016+) cars use revised parts. Inspect for coolant residue around the engine valley.

Shared across the JLR range — the 8HP's mechatronic unit can develop solenoid faults, causing harsh shifts or transmission faults. Not engine-specific but frequently enquired alongside engine problems.

marketIntelligence.h2: F-Type Market Intelligence — What 50+ UK Owners Told Us in 2025
insights: Interesting insight: The F-Type's 53 enquiries in 2025 make it Jaguar's lowest-volume model in the dataset — but its enquiry-to-value ratio is among the highest, with average host values of £18,000–£65,000 making replacement economics unusually safe compared to lower-value Jags.

The V6-to-straight-six transition is barely visible in the data. F-Type enquiries are overwhelmingly for the AJ126 V6 (560 across all models) — but this figure is aggregated across all AJ126-equipped Jags, not F-Type-specific. The AJ20D6 straight-six is too recent and too low-volume in F-Type fitment to generate meaningful proprietary data yet.

The F-Type's cooling-system failure pattern dominates the conversation. Across AJ126 and AJ133 enquiries, cooling system (crossover pipes, water pumps) and timing-chain tensioner (pre-2013 units) are the most frequently mentioned symptoms. A buyer of any pre-2019 F-Type should budget £1,000–£2,000 for cooling-system preventative work unless full history shows it's been done.

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every F-Type Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: Buy a 2019+ car with the AJ20D6 straight-six if you want the cleanest reliability record — it trades the V6's cooling-system worries for a modern turbocharged six with no systemic failure patterns. If you must have the V8, buy a 2016+ car with documented cooling-system history and budget £1,500–£2,000 for preventative work. The V6 sounds glorious, but pre-2019 cooling system maintenance is a 'when', not an 'if'. End of Sections 1-7 — ready for Sections 8-14.

replacementCosts.h2: F-Type Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for every F-Type engine — used, reconditioned and rebuilt, supply-only, with independent labour guidance.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting an F-Type engine typically takes 12–18 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [JAG-QUOTE]. The F-Type rule — economics vs soundtrack: An F-Type's typical value of £18,000–£65,000 means even the AJ133's upper reconditioned cost of £9,000 sits at 15–50% of vehicle value — well below our 60% threshold for Jaguar. The F-Type is one of the safest Jaguars to replace an engine on, purely because its retained values comfortably absorb the cost. The only caveat: if your F-Type is a high-mileage, early-register car worth £10,000–£15,000, a £9,000 AJ133 rebuild starts to approach 60–90% of vehicle value. In that scenario, evaluate the car's condition, history and mileage before committing — a low-mileage example is always worth saving; a tired one might not be.

engineEvolution.h2: How the F-Type Engine Range Evolved — 2013 to Today

eras.whyBmwChanged: Jaguar needed a sports car to follow the XK — the F-Type launched with the AJ-V8 family already well-established from the XK and XJ.
eras.worthKnowing: The AJ126 is essentially an AJ133 V8 with two cylinders removed — same architecture, same supercharger layout, same cooling-system design.

eras.keyEngines: AJ20D6 3.0 I6 Turbo (P300/P340/P400), AJ133 5.0 V8 SC (revised)
eras.whyBmwChanged: JLR's Ingenium straight-six replaced the AJ126 V6 across the range — emissions, efficiency, and a clean-sheet design with no cooling-system weaknesses.
eras.worthKnowing: The AJ20D6 is the same engine family as the Defender's D300 and Range Rover's P400 — a modular straight-six with no systemic failure patterns.

eras.whyBmwChanged: Jaguar's transition to all-electric by 2025 meant the F-Type's days were numbered — the V8 carried on in reduced volumes for enthusiasts.
eras.worthKnowing: The F-Type was discontinued in 2024 with no direct successor — the last of Jaguar's internal-combustion sports cars.

profiles.verdict: The F-Type's supercharged V8 delivers one of the best sports-car soundtracks of any modern car. The 575bhp SVR is the one to hunt — but budget £1,500–£2,000 for cooling-system preventative work on any pre-2019 car.
The F-Type is at its best on a Sunday B-road. The V6 is sweeter than the V8 for pure handling balance — the 380bhp V6 is the enthusiast's sweet spot if you can live with the cooling-system caveats.

The F-Type is a sports car, not a grand tourer. The ride is firm, the boot is small, and the running costs are real. If you're commuting daily in an F-Type, buy a 2019+ car with the AJ20D6 — it's the most civilised and the most reliable.

The F-Type is the last of Jaguar's internal-combustion sports cars — that alone makes it a future classic. A low-mileage, early-register V8 SVR with full history is the long-term keeper. The AJ20D6 cars are better to own and drive, but the V8 will be the collector's choice.

The F-Type is a surprisingly approachable sports car — the V6 is manageable, the chassis is forgiving, and the drama is instantly addictive. Buy a 2019+ car with the AJ20D6: no cooling-system worries, modern infotainment, and the cleanest reliability record in the range.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My F-Type Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: The F-Type's AJ126 V6 cooling-system failures are the most common repair — if the plastic crossover pipes have cracked and you've caught it early, a repair is usually viable. If it has overheated and warped the heads, replacement is often more cost-effective. The AJ133 V8 follows the same logic — cooling system repairs are routine, but a full rebuild is serious money.

signals.title: Built on Real Data
signals.text: Every reliability rating and cost range for the F-Type comes from genuine UK quote and enquiry data — 50+ F-Type enquiries in 2025 [JAG-VERIFIED], cross-referenced against 560 AJ126 and 330 AJ133 enquiries across the Jaguar range — not manufacturer marketing, not forum guesswork.

signals.title: We Tell You When Not to Repair
signals.text: The F-Type is a rare Jaguar where replacement almost always makes sense — the car's value comfortably absorbs even the AJ133's upper reconditioned cost of £9,000, sitting at 15–50% of vehicle value. We only flag caution when a high-mileage early car's value drops below £15,000 and the repair cost approaches 60%.

signals.title: Vetted Jaguar Specialists
signals.text: Our network of 15+ vetted Jaguar specialists includes technicians who work on F-Types daily — they know the AJ126's cooling-system weak points, the AJ133's chain-tensioner history, and the AJ20D6's modern engineering.

signals.title: Part of Engine Finders
